title.zh: 價錢背後的隱形階級：為什麼富人更認同「風險定價」？
title.en: Whose Side Are You On? How Wealth Shapes Our Views on Fair Pricing

summary.zh: 你有過被保險公司拒絕保單的經驗嗎？或貸款申請時被標註為『高風險客戶』？這些看似『理所當然』的市場規則，背後竟隱藏著階級的暗流。美國社會學家近期研究發現：當我們評判『風險定價』是否公平時，富人比窮人更傾向支持這種『誰 risky 誰付錢』的邏輯——即使這可能損及自身利益。三項大規模調查顯示，高收入者對『預測性定價』的道德認同度，明顯高於低收入群體，連政治立場都無法解釋這現象。研究者提出『選擇性共情』概念：富裕者更願意站在企業立場思考，把高風險消費者當成『自己人』的對立面。這就像給公司穿上了心理防護盾，讓市場規則成為階級優勢的隱形工具。原來，我們對公平的判斷從未客觀，而是被收入階層悄悄綁定了價值觀。
summary.en: Have you ever been denied insurance or labeled a 'high-risk' loan applicant? These seemingly routine market practices reveal a hidden class divide. A recent study in the American Sociological Review uncovers how Americans judge risk-based pricing—where companies charge more for those predicted to be costly—not as neutral economics but through a moral lens shaped by wealth. Contrary to expectations, higher-income individuals are significantly more likely than lower-income groups to endorse the fairness of such pricing, even when it contradicts their self-interest. The researchers introduce 'selective empathy' as the key mechanism: wealthier people disproportionately align with corporate perspectives, viewing high-risk consumers as outsiders rather than fellow citizens. This isn’t about ideology—it’s about emotional orientation. The findings expose how risk-based pricing becomes a classed form of economic rationality, where empathy for the customer is sidelined by privilege. In a system where fairness is decided by whose side you stand on, the rich aren’t just paying more—they’re defining what 'fair' means.

keyArticle.title: Seeing Like a Company or a Customer: Selective Empathy in Pricing
